title: Get started with arcgeocoder
text: arcgeocoder accesses ArcGIS REST API without requiring API key additional HTTP package curl. uses base R download functions, keeps dependency footprint small. package provides focused interfaces findAddressCandidates reverseGeocode endpoints. supports single-line addresses, structured address components, category filters reverse geocoding.

title: Get started with arcgeocoder
text: following packages provide related geocoding features: tidygeocoder (Cambon et al. 2021) provides interface ArcGIS, Nominatim (OpenStreetMap), Google, TomTom, Mapbox geocoding services. nominatimlite (Hernangómez 2024) similar arcgeocoder uses data OpenStreetMap Nominatim API.

title: Get started with arcgeocoder
text: examples section adapted tidygeocoder package. arc_geo() function converts single-line addresses geographic coordinates. requires API key additional setup. default, arc_geo() returns small set fields. Set full_results = TRUE return available API fields. Table 1: Example: geocoding addresses. arc_reverse_geo() function converts longitude latitude values addresses. Supply longitude values x latitude values y. following example uses coordinates returned previous query. address argument sets name address column output. Table 2: Example: reverse geocoding addresses. arc_geo_categories() function finds places category near location within bounding box. Available categories documented arc_categories dataset ArcGIS category filtering documentation. following example finds food-related places, restaurants, coffee shops bakeries, near Eiffel Tower France.  Example: Food places near Eiffel Tower
code: library(arcgeocoder) library(dplyr)  # Create a data frame with addresses. some_addresses <- tribble(   ~name, ~addr,   "White House", "1600 Pennsylvania Ave NW, Washington, DC",   "Transamerica Pyramid", "600 Montgomery St, San Francisco, CA 94111",   "Willis Tower", "233 S Wacker Dr, Chicago, IL 60606" )  # Geocode the addresses. lat_longs <- arc_geo(   some_addresses$addr,   lat = "latitude",   long = "longitude",   progressbar = FALSE ) reverse <- arc_reverse_geo(   x = lat_longs$longitude,   y = lat_longs$latitude,   address = "address_found",   progressbar = FALSE ) library(ggplot2) # For plotting.  # Step 1: Locate the Eiffel Tower using a structured query.  eiffel_tower <- arc_geo_multi(   address = "Tour Eiffel",   city = "Paris",   countrycode = "FR",   langcode = "FR",   custom_query = list(outFields = "LongLabel") )  # Display results. eiffel_tower |>   select(lon, lat, LongLabel) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>     lon   lat LongLabel                                                                    #>   <dbl> <dbl> <chr>                                                                        #> 1  2.29  48.9 Tour Eiffel, 3 Rue de l'Université, 75007, 7e Arrondissement, Paris, Île-de…  # Use `lon` and `lat` as a reference location for `category = "Food"`. food_eiffel <- arc_geo_categories(   "Food",   x = eiffel_tower$lon,   y = eiffel_tower$lat,   limit = 50,   full_results = TRUE )  # Plot by food type. ggplot(eiffel_tower, aes(x, y)) +   geom_point(shape = 15, color = "blue", size = 4) +   geom_point(data = food_eiffel, aes(x, y, color = Type)) +   labs(     title = "Food near the Eiffel Tower",     subtitle = "Using arcgeocoder",     color = "Type of place",     x = "",     y = "",     caption = "Data from the ArcGIS REST API"   )

what: How reverse geocoding selects a feature
title: Reverse geocoding and feature types
text: Reverse geocoding converts longitude latitude values address. ArcGIS REST API reverseGeocode operation selects relevant feature near input location using prioritized hierarchy feature types. following table lists hierarchy descending order priority. Unless otherwise noted, feature type returned distance input location feature within tolerance specified Search tolerance column. Table 1: Adapted ArcGIS REST API reverseGeocode arcgeocoder, featuretypes argument arc_reverse_geo() controls feature types API can return. default featuretypes = NULL, request filter feature types full hierarchy Table 1 applies. Use featuretypes restrict results one following values: "StreetInt" "DistanceMarker" "StreetAddress" "StreetName" "POI" "Subaddress" "PointAddress" "Postal" "Locality" Supply multiple feature types character vector. arcgeocoder converts vector comma-separated value required API.
code: arc_reverse_geo(..., featuretypes = "PointAddress") arc_reverse_geo(..., featuretypes = c("PointAddress", "StreetAddress"))

title: Reverse geocoding and feature types
code: arc_reverse_geo(..., featuretypes = "PointAddress")

title: Reverse geocoding and feature types
code: arc_reverse_geo(..., featuretypes = c("PointAddress", "StreetAddress"))

title: Reverse geocoding and feature types
text: following examples show featuretypes affects reverse geocoding results. example uses default featuretypes = NULL, API selects result full hierarchy Table 1. Addr_type field identifies returned feature type. Set featuretypes = "StreetAddress" restrict request street addresses. Set featuretypes = "Locality" restrict request locality features. request includes multiple values, API applies hierarchy Table 1 requested feature types. feature types available near certain locations. reverse geocoding North Pole, API returns Locality find StreetAddress. result available, arc_reverse_geo() returns tibble requested address column set NA.
code: library(arcgeocoder) library(dplyr) example_x <- -117.196324 example_y <- 34.059217  api_poi <- arc_reverse_geo(   x = example_x,   y = example_y,   langcode = "EN",   full_results = TRUE,   verbose = TRUE )  api_poi |>   select(x, y, address, lon, lat, Addr_type) |>   knitr::kable() api_address <- arc_reverse_geo(   x = example_x,   y = example_y,   featuretypes = "StreetAddress",   langcode = "EN",   full_results = TRUE,   verbose = TRUE )  api_address |>   select(x, y, address, lon, lat, Addr_type) |>   knitr::kable() api_local <- arc_reverse_geo(   x = example_x,   y = example_y,   featuretypes = "Locality",   langcode = "EN",   full_results = TRUE,   verbose = TRUE )  api_local |>   select(x, y, address, lon, lat, Addr_type) |>   knitr::kable() api_multiple <- arc_reverse_geo(   x = example_x,   y = example_y,   featuretypes = c("Locality", "StreetInt", "StreetAddress"),   langcode = "EN",   full_results = TRUE,   verbose = TRUE )  api_multiple |>   select(x, y, address, lon, lat, Addr_type) |>   knitr::kable() # Reverse geocode the North Pole.  npole <- arc_reverse_geo(x = 0, y = 90, langcode = "EN", full_results = TRUE)  npole |>   select(x, y, address, lon, lat, Addr_type) |>   knitr::kable() # Request a feature type that is unavailable. npole2 <- arc_reverse_geo(   x = 0,   y = 90,   langcode = "EN",   full_results = TRUE,   featuretypes = "StreetAddress" )  npole2 |>   knitr::kable()

title: Interactive maps with arcgeocoder
text: example displays results arcgeocoder interactive leaflet map. crosstalk links map filterable reactable table.
code:
# Coffee shops and bakeries around the Eiffel Tower. library(arcgeocoder) library(leaflet) library(dplyr) library(reactable) library(crosstalk) # Step 1: Locate the Eiffel Tower. eiffel_tower <- arc_geo_multi("Eiffel Tower", city = "Paris", countrycode = "FR", category = "POI" ) # Base URL for icons. icon_url <- paste0( "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dieghernan/arcgeocoder/", "main/vignettes/articles/" ) eiffel_icon <- makeIcon( iconUrl = paste0(icon_url, "eiffel-tower.png"), iconWidth = 50, iconHeight = 50, iconAnchorX = 25, iconAnchorY = 25 ) # Step 2: Find nearby coffee shops and bakeries. cf_bk <- arc_geo_categories( category = c("Coffee Shop", "Bakery"), x = eiffel_tower$lon, y = eiffel_tower$lat, limit = 50, full_results = TRUE ) # Create labels and icons. labs <- paste0("", cf_bk$PlaceName, "
", cf_bk$StAddr) # Assign icons. leaf_icons <- icons( ifelse(cf_bk$Type == "Coffee Shop", paste0(icon_url, "coffee-cup.png"), paste0(icon_url, "croissant.png") ), iconWidth = 20, iconHeight = 20, iconAnchorX = 10, iconAnchorY = 10 ) # Step 3: Create a shared data object. cf_bk_data <- cf_bk |> select(Place = ShortLabel, Type, Address = Place_addr, City, URL, Phone) |> SharedData$new(group = "Food") # Step 4: Create a leaflet map with crosstalk. # Initialize the leaflet map. lmend <- leaflet( data = cf_bk_data, elementId = "EiffelTower", width = "100%", height = "60vh", options = leafletOptions(minZoom = 12) ) |> setView(eiffel_tower$lon, eiffel_tower$lat, zoom = 16) |> addProviderTiles( provider = "CartoDB.Positron", group = "CartoDB.Positron" ) |> addTiles(group = "OSM") |> addMarkers(data = eiffel_tower, ~lon, ~lat, icon = eiffel_icon) |> addMarkers( lat = cf_bk$lat, lng = cf_bk$lon, popup = labs, icon = leaf_icons ) |> addLayersControl( baseGroups = c("CartoDB.Positron", "OSM"), position = "topleft", options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE) ) # Step 5: Create a filterable table. tb <- reactable(cf_bk_data, selection = "multiple", onClick = "select", rowStyle = list(cursor = "pointer"), filterable = TRUE, searchable = TRUE, showPageSizeOptions = TRUE, striped = TRUE, defaultColDef = colDef(vAlign = "center", minWidth = 150), paginationType = "jump", elementId = "coffees", columns = list( Place = colDef( sticky = "left", rowHeader = TRUE, name = "", cell = function(value) { htmltools::strong(value) } ), URL = colDef(cell = function(value) { # Render as a link. if (any(is.null(value), is.na(value))) { return("") } htmltools::a(href = value, target = "_blank", as.character(value)) }), Phone = colDef(cell = function(value) { # Render as a link. if (any(is.null(value), is.na(value))) { return("") } clearphone <- gsub("-", "", value, fixed = TRUE) clearphone <- gsub(" ", "", clearphone, fixed = TRUE) htmltools::a( href = paste0("tel:", clearphone), target = "_blank", as.character(value) ) }) ) )

title: Static maps with arcgeocoder
text: example converts results arcgeocoder sf object displays static map. Figure 1: map showing location McDonald’s restaurants around Barcelona, Spain Figure 2: map showing location McDonald’s restaurants Barcelona, Spain
code: library(arcgeocoder) library(dplyr) library(sf) # Work with spatial objects. library(ggplot2) library(mapSpain) # Get municipality boundaries for Spain.  # McDonald's restaurants in Barcelona, Spain.  mc <- arc_geo_multi(   "McDonalds",   city = "Barcelona",   region = "Catalonia",   countrycode = "ES",   category = "Food",   limit = 50,   custom_query = list(outFields = c("LongLabel", "Type", "StAddr")) )  # Convert to an sf object. mc_sf <- st_as_sf(   mc,   coords = c("lon", "lat"),   # Use the WKID from the geocoding results.   crs = mc$latestWkid[1] )  bcn <- esp_get_munic(munic = "Barcelona") |>   st_transform(mc$latestWkid[1])  ggplot(bcn) +   geom_sf() +   geom_sf(data = mc_sf, color = "red") # Restrict results to the Barcelona bounding box in the query. bbox <- st_bbox(bcn) |> paste0(collapse = ",") bbox #> [1] "2.0536216,41.3217545,2.227167,41.467717"  mc2_sf <- arc_geo_multi(   "McDonalds",   city = "Barcelona",   region = "Catalonia",   countrycode = "ES",   category = "Food",   limit = 50,   custom_query = list(     outFields = c("LongLabel", "Type", "StAddr"),     searchExtent = bbox   ) ) |>   st_as_sf(coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = mc$latestWkid[1])  ggplot(bcn) +   geom_sf() +   geom_sf(data = mc2_sf, color = "red")

title: Static maps with arcgeocoder
text: maptiles package retrieves static map tiles terra objects. tidyterra package adds tiles ggplot2 map. Figure 3: map showing location McDonald’s restaurants Barcelona, Spain, image provided CARTO
code: library(maptiles) library(tidyterra)  # Use EPSG:3857 to retrieve tiles. bcn_3857 <- st_transform(bcn, 3857)  osm_tiles <- get_tiles(bcn_3857, provider = "CartoDB.Positron", crop = TRUE)  ggplot() +   geom_spatraster_rgb(data = osm_tiles, maxcell = Inf) +   geom_sf(data = bcn, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 1) +   geom_sf(data = mc2_sf, color = "red") +   coord_sf(crs = 3857) +   labs(caption = get_credit("CartoDB.Positron"))

code: library(arcgeocoder) library(dplyr)  # Create a data frame with addresses. some_addresses <- tribble(   ~name, ~addr,   "White House", "1600 Pennsylvania Ave NW, Washington, DC",   "Transamerica Pyramid", "600 Montgomery St, San Francisco, CA 94111",   "Willis Tower", "233 S Wacker Dr, Chicago, IL 60606" )  # Geocode the addresses. lat_longs <- arc_geo(   some_addresses$addr,   lat = "latitude",   long = "longitude",   progressbar = FALSE ) reverse <- arc_reverse_geo(   x = lat_longs$longitude,   y = lat_longs$latitude,   address = "address_found",   progressbar = FALSE ) library(ggplot2) # For plotting.  # Step 1: Locate the Eiffel Tower using a structured query.  eiffel_tower <- arc_geo_multi(   address = "Tour Eiffel",   city = "Paris",   countrycode = "FR",   langcode = "FR",   custom_query = list(outFields = "LongLabel") )  # Display results. eiffel_tower |>   select(lon, lat, LongLabel) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>     lon   lat LongLabel                                                          #>   <dbl> <dbl> <chr>                                                              #> 1  2.29  48.9 Tour Eiffel, 3 Rue de l'Université, 75007, 7e Arrondissement, Par…  # Use `lon` and `lat` as a reference location for `category = "Food"`. food_eiffel <- arc_geo_categories(   "Food",   x = eiffel_tower$lon,   y = eiffel_tower$lat,   limit = 50,   full_results = TRUE )  # Plot by food type. ggplot(eiffel_tower, aes(x, y)) +   geom_point(shape = 15, color = "blue", size = 4) +   geom_point(data = food_eiffel, aes(x, y, color = Type)) +   labs(     title = "Food near the Eiffel Tower",     subtitle = "Using arcgeocoder",     color = "Type of place",     x = "",     y = "",     caption = "Data from the ArcGIS REST API"   )

text: See ArcGIS REST API category filtering details examples. ArcGIS geocoding service supports searches many types addresses places around world, applications need anticipate types places users may search . However, ambiguous searches can match many different places produce unexpected results. example, search city may match street name, airport code may match country abbreviation. cases, category argument can filter unwanted results. limits types places service searches , can eliminate false-positive matches speed search. dataset lists categories three hierarchy levels (level_1, level_2 level_3). level_1 category requested (example, POI), child categories may also included results.

code: # \donttest{ # Full workflow: gas stations near Carabanchel, Madrid.  # Geocode Carabanchel. carab <- arc_geo("Carabanchel, Madrid, Spain")  # Extract the CRS. carab_crs <- unique(carab$latestWkid)  library(ggplot2)  base_map <- ggplot(carab) +   geom_point(aes(lon, lat), size = 5, color = "red") +   geom_rect(aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = ymin, ymax = ymax),     fill = NA,     color = "blue"   ) +   coord_sf(crs = carab_crs)  # Example 1: Search near Carabanchel (not restricted). ex1 <- arc_geo_categories("Gas Station",   # Use Carabanchel as the search origin.   x = carab$lon, y = carab$lat,   limit = 50, full_results = TRUE )  # Reduce labels to the most common ones. library(dplyr)  labs <- ex1 |>   count(ShortLabel) |>   slice_max(n = 7, order_by = n) |>   pull(ShortLabel)  base_map +   geom_point(data = ex1, aes(lon, lat, color = ShortLabel)) +   scale_color_discrete(breaks = labs) +   labs(     title = "Example 1",     subtitle = "Search near (points may be far away)"   )   # Example 2: Include part of the name for different results. ex2 <- arc_geo_categories("Gas Station",   # Match this name.   name = "Repsol",   # Use Carabanchel as the search origin.   x = carab$lon, y = carab$lat,   limit = 50, full_results = TRUE )  base_map +   geom_point(data = ex2, aes(lon, lat, color = ShortLabel)) +   labs(     title = "Example 2",     subtitle = "Search near with name"   )   # Example 3: Search within a bounding box. ex3 <- arc_geo_categories("Gas Station",   name = "Repsol",   bbox = c(carab$xmin, carab$ymin, carab$xmax, carab$ymax),   limit = 50, full_results = TRUE )  base_map +   geom_point(data = ex3, aes(lon, lat, color = ShortLabel)) +   labs(     title = "Example 3",     subtitle = "Search near with name and bounding box"   )  # }

title: Geocode addresses with a structured ArcGIS REST API query — arc_geo_multi
text: function performs structured queries using separate address components. least one component must NA NULL. argument can vector geocode multiple addresses. Vectors supplied different arguments must length. following list provides brief description argument: address: string representing first line street address. usually contains street name house number, can also contain building place name. address2: string represents second line street address. can include street name house number, building name, place name subunit. address3: string represents third line street address. can include street name house number, building name, place name subunit. neighborhood: smallest administrative division associated address, typically neighborhood section larger populated place. city: next largest administrative division associated address, typically city municipality. subregion: next largest administrative division associated address. Depending country, subregion can represent county, state province. region: largest administrative division associated address, typically state province. postal: standard postal code address, typically three- six-character alphanumeric code. postalext: postal code extension, United States Postal Service ZIP+4 code. countrycode: value representing country. Providing value can increase search speed. Acceptable values include full country name English official language country, two-character country code three-character country code.

text: x numeric vector longitude values range \(\left[-180, 180 \right]\). y numeric vector latitude values range \(\left[-90, 90 \right]\). address Name address column output. default "address". full_results logical value indicating whether return available API fields. default, FALSE, returns latitude, longitude address. return_coords logical value indicating whether return input coordinates results. verbose logical value indicating whether display API request details. progressbar logical value indicating whether display progress bar multiple queries. outsr Spatial reference output coordinates. default NULL, uses service default. See Details arc_spatial_references. langcode Language returned addresses. featuretypes character vector limits possible match types. default NULL, filter feature type. See Details. locationtype Location represented output geometry featuretypes "PointAddress" "Subaddress". Valid values "rooftop" "street". default NULL. custom_query named list additional API parameters.
